name: French B2 Vocabulary
description: Upper-intermediate French — CEFR B2 level vocabulary

facts.explanation: abaisser — to lower; to reduce; to bring down. Also: to come or go down; to descend, to humble oneself, demean oneself, stoop; abase, degrade, humiliate, to roll out (the dough)
abandon — surrender; desertion; withdrawal. Also: abandonment, abandoning, state of neglect, abandon, unrestraint (yielding to natural impulses or inhibitions; freedom from artificial constraint)
abasourdir — to dumbfound; to baffle; to astonish. Also: to stun or daze with a loud sound
abattage — slaughter or butchering for meat. Also: felling or cutting down of (trees), destruction, demolition of (a wall)
abattoir — abattoir, slaughterhouse (place where animals are slaughtered). Also: abattoir, slaughterhouse (place or event likened to a slaughterhouse, because of great carnage or bloodshed)

abattre — to butcher; to slaughter for meat. Also: to shoot dead, to cut down (a tree), to destroy or demolish (a wall), to fall down, especially of tall things, such as trees, to descend upon with violence or furor, to strike
abeille — bee, honey bee. Also: a writer whose style is considered pure like honey, bullet
aboiement — bark (sound made by a dog).
abondance — a large amount; a plethora or profusion. Also: wealth of goods, abundance; opulence, prosperity, abundance
abonner — to subscribe somebody to a service; to take a subscription for. Also: to subscribe to a service, publication, or social media account., to become accustomed to or a regular of
abonné — subscriber. Also: follower, a household for the purpose of utility delivery, season ticket holder
abord — the manner with which one acts in the presence of another person or persons, especially in a first encounter. Also: the surroundings of a place, arrival or accessibility by water
abréger — to shorten in duration. Also: to shorten (in textual length) or summarize, to abridge, to abbreviate
absenter — to leave; to absent oneself.
abstraire — to abstract (to separate; to remove; to take away).
abuser — to mislead. Also: to take advantage [with de ‘of’] (especially sexually), to abuse (use improperly), to go too far
abîme — abyss, chasm. Also: bottom of a chasm or valley, infiniteness of thought, superlative used with various abstraction and qualities; the climax or acme, hell, abyss of a shield, fess point
abîmer — to damage. Also: to fall down or sink and disappear in a hole, depth, abyss, to become absorbed in something (thought, action), to throw down in a hole, depth etc.
accaparer — to monopolize, to corner. Also: to occupy, to grab, to acquire, to arrogate (often with force or money), to engross, to preoccupy
accentuer — to accentuate (emphasize). Also: to intensify, to accent (put an accent on a letter or symbol etc.)
acception — meaning, sense (of a word), acceptation.
accessible — accessible, attainable, obtainable, available. Also: affordable, approachable
accidenter — to damage; to hurt (in an accident).
acclamer — to acclaim, applaud (to shout applause).
accoucher — to go into labour. Also: to give birth to, to deliver (a baby), to help someone deliver (a baby), to come up with, to say, tell something that one hid beforehand
accourir — to rush up, run up, hurry.
accroître — to increase. Also: to increase, to grow
accélérateur — accelerator, gas pedal. Also: accelerator
acharnement — relentlessness, doggedness. Also: persecution, harassment
acharner — to incite; to sic. Also: to hound, to fiercely attack, to slave away, to persevere, to attach oneself excessively
acheminer — to transport; send (off). Also: to head (towards); head for; make one's way (to)
achever — to finish, to complete. Also: to finish off (someone who is already incapacitated), to finish
acide — acid, acidic, sour, tart. Also: acid, acidic
acquitter — to pay. Also: to acknowledge (a signal), to notify the receipt of, to clear, to acquit, to discharge, to complete (one's duty)
actif — asset. Also: active, top, An active ingredient, in a perfume, experiment, etc.
actionner — to work (to set into action), actuate, action.

activer — to activate (to encourage development or induce increased activity; to stimulate). Also: to activate (to put a device, mechanism or system into action or motion; to enable), to activate (to render radioactive), to speed up, to stoke (e.g. a fire)
adhérer — to adhere; to stick to (rules, regulations). Also: to belong to; to be part of; to be a member of, to associate, to join
adjoindre — to adjoin. Also: to join up

adonner — to devote oneself. Also: to get along well, be compatible, to get used to, to happen, come to pass (by chance), to happen (by coincidence), (of the wind) to blow in a favorable direction, allowing the sails to luff
affaiblir — to weaken, to flag, to make weaker, to enfeeble, enervate, attenuate. Also: to weaken, to become weaker
affairer — to absorb. Also: to busy oneself
affamer — to starve (somebody or something, like a city during a siege).
affectation — allocation, allotment. Also: assignment, posting, affectation
affecter — to feign, affect (an emotion, etc.). Also: to allocate (something),, to assign someone, to post someone, to affect, influence
affection — affection, love, fondness. Also: medical condition, complaint, disease
affectionner — to affectionate, to feel affection for, to be fond of.
affiner — to purify. Also: to refine, to make finer
affliger — to grieve someone, to distress someone. Also: to smite
affolement — panic, terror. Also: insanity, craziness, lunacy
affranchir — to free, liberate, enfranchise, emancipate. Also: to discharge, exempt, to prepay (postage), frank (a letter), to pervert, to inform, to release or free oneself
affreusement — dreadfully (in a dreadful manner), horribly.
affût — gun carriage. Also: mount, tripod
agacer — to irritate. Also: to tease
agenda — organiser, appointment book, engagement book.
agglomération — agglomeration. Also: village, town, any built-up area
aggraver — to worsen (to make worse). Also: to worsen (to become worse)
agile — nimble, agile (quick and light in movement or action).
agneau — lamb (young sheep). Also: lamb (sheep meat), lambskin
aigle — eagle (any of a number of species of birds of prey). Also: a man of ingenuity and superior talent; a genius
aigu — sharp, pointy. Also: acute, keen, discerning, sharp, loud and high-pitched, acute, front
aiguiser — to sharpen (of a point, to make sharper). Also: to hone (e.g. a skill), to whet
aire — (surface) area. Also: a flat surface, direction of the wind, threshing floor, area, zone, range (a space in which a certain thing occurs)

aisé — easy to do, with ease, simple, effortless. Also: wealthy, with no money problems, affluent, prosperous, on easy street, in easy circumstances, smooth, fluid (about a movement or a conversation), loose, big
alimenter — to feed, to give food to. Also: to supply, to fund, to fuel, feed (e.g. an argument), to eat, to feed

aller — to go. Also: to attend (school, church regularly), to be going (to); will soon; forms a near-future tense, to be (feeling), to go well, to suit, to be X-proof (to be suitable for use in an appliance without running the risk of being damaged in the process)
alliance — alliance, union. Also: wedding ring
allier — to ally. Also: to alloy
allumage — lighting. Also: switching on, turning on
allumette — match (small flammable object). Also: shoestring French fry
allure — appearance, look. Also: speed, pace, angle of a boat from the wind, gait (of a horse), chemin de ronde (raised protected walkway behind a castle battlement)

allée — path, lane, allée. Also: hallway (leading from the entrance of a house), aisle (between rows of seats), driveway, bowling lane, action of going (used nowadays only in the expression allées et venues: the repeated action of going and returning)
alléger — to lighten. Also: to alleviate, relieve, to reduce
alphabétisation — elimination of illiteracy, the process of learning to write and to read (and to compute).
altérer — to alter; change; fiddle with. Also: to cause thirst
aléatoire — doubtful, dubious, uncertain, unpredictable. Also: random, aleatory
amant — lover (one who loves a particular person). Also: lover (one who loves a particular thing, pursuit, etc.), lover (participant in an extramarital or otherwise illicit romantic or sexual affair)
ambigu — ambiguation. Also: a meal where all courses are served together; a buffet
ambiguïté — ambiguity (something liable to more than one interpretation).

amorcer — to prime, set in motion. Also: to begin, to get to work on (to commence a project), to break ground (start construction work), to boot, to bait (lace with bait), to bait, chum (try to attract fish with bait), to bait in, to string in, reel in, to bait (e.g. an enemy, to react)
ampleur — scale, size, extent, scope (of problem, project, deficit etc.). Also: volume (of sound), fullness, opulence, liberalness (of style etc.)
ampoule — light bulb. Also: ampoule, blister, pimple, zit
aménagement — fitting out (of a place). Also: laying out (of ground), development (of a district), planning, adjusting
ancrer — to anchor. Also: to fix, to anchor, to make permanent
andouillette — Type of sausage that is eaten grilled and hot, made from elements of the pig's digestive tract (usually the large intestine and stomach)..
angoisse — anguish, distress. Also: anxiety, angst, fear, dread
angoisser — to stress, to worry; to cause panic or fear.
annuaire — annual; year book (yearly publication). Also: directory
annuler — to cancel (to invalidate, to annul).
antan — last year. Also: yesteryear, the old days, yore
antenne — a yard supporting a lateen sail.. Also: antenna (feeler organ on the head of an insect), antenna (equipment to pick up an electrical signal), branch, outlet
antillais — Antillean (of, from or relating to Antilles).
antique — ancient. Also: of the Antiquity
anéantir — to wipe out, to destroy, to shatter. Also: to hit someone for six
apogée — apogee (a point in an orbit around the Earth).
apparition — appearance. Also: ghost, plate appearance
appelant — appellant. Also: a decoy for birdhunt
appellation — call (instance of calling out). Also: a common name; appellation

appliquer — to apply, to put something on something else. Also: to apply, to employ, to make use of, to apply to, to be applicable to, to map to, to apply one's effort to something, to try hard, to dedicate oneself
apposer — to put up, stick up, attach. Also: to stamp, sign
apprivoiser — to tame. Also: to win over, to charm
approfondir — to deepen. Also: to consider more thoroughly, to delve deeper into
approprier — to adapt (something) to a situation. Also: to seize, take, to make one's own
approvisionner — to supply; stock. Also: to shop
appréhender — to dread, apprehend. Also: to catch, apprehend
apprêter — to prepare, to get ready, prime. Also: to prepare for/to do, to get ready for/to do, to be about to do
apéro — apéritif (alcoholic drink served before a meal as an appetiser). Also: apéritif (time before a meal when apéritifs are drunk)
arborer — to sport (clothes, a badge etc.). Also: to wear (a facial expression e.g. a smile, a frown), to fly, display (a flag)
arc — bow (weapon). Also: arc (curve), arc, circular arc, circle segment, arch, story arc
archives — archive, the historical material (usually documents) considered as a whole. Also: place for storing archives, plural of archive
archéologie — archaeology (scientific study of past remains).
archéologique — archaeological (relating to the science or research of archaeology).
ardeur — intense heat. Also: ardour, fervour, passion
ardu — steep (of a near-vertical gradient). Also: arduous, needing or using up much energy, difficult
argenté — silver (having a color/colour like silver), silvery. Also: silvered, wealthy
arguer — to deduce (arrive at a conclusion). Also: to argue
argumenter — to present one's arguments, to set out one's arguments, to argue.
aride — arid (very dry). Also: dry (free from or lacking embellishment or sweetness)
arrachement — uprooting. Also: extracting, extraction, snatching, grabbing

arracher — to uproot, pull up, tear out. Also: to extract, take out (a tooth), to pull off, rip off, peel, to buy, snap up, to fight over (something), to scram; to get out, to tear away; to tear apart
arrière — far, far away. Also: late, rear, behind, hinter-, great- (relatives)
arrondir — to round (make round). Also: to round up, round down or round off
artère — artery (“blood vessel”). Also: arterial, thoroughfare
as — ace (card of value 1). Also: ace (expert or pilot), as (Roman coin)
asile — asylum (place of safety or refuge). Also: retirement home, old people's home, asylum (psychiatric institution), protection, defense
aspirer — to aspire (to wait, to long). Also: to aspirate (to produce an audible puff of breath. especially following a consonant), to inhale, to breathe in or out, to extract, to suck up
assassinat — assassination. Also: first-degree murder (premeditated murder)

assemblage — assemblage, gathering, assembly (process of assembling; result of this action). Also: jointing (act of making a joint), joint, a phase in compilation where an assembly language is translated into a binary file, cuvée (wine produced from a mixture of several grape varieties), assemblage (group of different artifacts found in association with one another), sequence assembly (aligning and merging of DNA fragments to reconstruct a sequence), genome assembly (process of reassembling DNA sequences), juxtaposition of several maps or cuttings to create a larger map
assimiler — to assimilate (all meanings), to absorb. Also: to liken, to lump together (e.g., one group with another), to digest (of food)

assise — foundation (supporting base of a wall). Also: base, basis, foundation, stratum, bed, layer (layer of sedimentary rock), stratum (layer of vegetation), stratum (layer of tissue), course (row or layer of bricks), seat of a chair
assiéger — to besiege; to lay siege to.
associatif — associative (algebraic property of an operator). Also: association; associative
assortir — to match, match up. Also: to pair up, to supply, stock up; to hook (someone) up with, to match

assouplir — to soften (make softer). Also: to make more supple, to relax (of e.g. a law or rule make less strict), to soften up, get soft (become softer), to become more supple, to become relaxed, relax (become less strict), to limber up
assouvir — to satisfy, assuage (hunger, passion, etc.).
assumer — embrace; accept; own. Also: assume (take on a role), take responsibility, synonym of présumer (“to assume; to presume”)
assureur — insurer, insurance agent. Also: belayer
assécher — to dry out; to dry.
astre — celestial body, astronomical object. Also: star
athlétique — athletic (having a muscular, well developed body, being in shape).
atome — atom (particle). Also: scrap, jot, iota, whit (very small amount)
attabler — to sit down at the table.
attarder — to delay; make late. Also: to linger; stay late
attentat — attack, assault (illegal act of violence toward another).
atterrir — to land (a plane, helicopter etc.).
atterrissage — landing on the ground; touchdown (of an aircraft).
attester — to attest; to support with evidence.
attirance — attraction (the state of feeling attraction). Also: attraction (the state of being attractive)
attribut — attribute (characteristic or quality). Also: complement, attribute
attribution — allocation, allotment. Also: remit, duty
aubain — a foreigner who was not naturalised, and who had few rights. Also: a foreigner or alien
aube — paddle, blade. Also: vane (of windmill), small plank
audace — audacity, boldness. Also: audacity, impudence
auditoire — audience, auditory. Also: court (people in the courtroom), auditorium (hall), lecture theatre, lecture hall
aune — ell. Also: measuring rod of one ell, yardstick (standard to which other comparisons are judged)
autochtone — from the earth, ground, soil. Also: autochthonous, native, indigenous, aboriginal, of indigenous peoples, or land occupied by them, natural, native, innate (to a person)
autonomie — autonomy. Also: range (distance a vehicle (car, byke, lorry, aircraft) can travel without refueling), battery life
avaler — to swallow. Also: to believe, to support, to approve, endorse, to swallow cum, to swallow
avancement — the state of advancing, progress. Also: a projection (i.e. from a building), a promotion, an advancement in status, clipping of avancement d'hoirie; an early inheritance
avancée — advance, progress. Also: an ordinance passed to hold a trial ahead of time
avant-garde — vanguard. Also: avant-garde, firing line, vanguard, the most politicized and conscious part of the proletariat.
avant-hier — two days ago; the day before yesterday.
aventurer — to venture. Also: to venture into
aveu — oath of allegiance. Also: confession
aveugler — to blind (make temporarily or permanently blind).
avide — avid, eager, desirous. Also: greedy, grasping
aviser — to advise. Also: to take stock of the situation and make a decision accordingly; to decide, to notice
avérer — to uncover, to reveal. Also: to prove (to be); to transpire
axer — to align on an axis. Also: to point, to orientate
aîné — eldest boy. Also: older brother, elder, old person
baby — table soccer, table football. Also: baby, darling, sweetheart, Mary Jane shoes
bailleur — a landowner who let uncultivated parcels of land to a lessee in a crop-sharing system in early Middle Ages. Also: a lessor in general
baisser — to lower. Also: to turn down (as a volume/setting), to go down, fall, to fail, to lower oneself; to bend over
balader — to stroll, walk around. Also: to give someone the runaround, to go for a walk, to trail

balai — broom. Also: broomstick flown by witches, the last bus or train (at night), tail (of a bird of prey), brush (electrical contact), blade (of windscreen wiper), year (used in referring to someone’s age)
balance — scales. Also: balance, drop-net, informant, snitch, the rest, the remainder, a scale, more specifically a balancing scale

balancer — to swing (to do a reciprocating movement). Also: to swing (to ride on a swing), to toss, to throw away, to discard, to denounce, to rat out on (to make a formal or public accusation against), to balance (to make the credits and debits of (an account) correspond)
balayer — to sweep. Also: to sweep aside, brush aside, to drag on the floor, to rake (sweep with gunfire), to make a clean sweep
baleine — whale (mammal). Also: whalebone, bone; stay (prop for clothing, in particular corsets), landwhale
baliser — to mark with beacons. Also: to waymark, to signpost, to prepare the ground, to tag
banaliser — to banalise. Also: to downplay, normalize, trivialize; to dismiss something unusual and serious as commonplace
bananier — any species of bananas relating to the genus Musa. Also: boat or ship used to transport bananas
bandeau — headband, bandeau. Also: parted hair, bandage, blindfold, a tight headband worn for a long time, usually from youth, for the ancient folk custom of cranial deformation
banderole — banner (in political demonstration). Also: banderole (in the sense of "streamer, little banner, little flag"), streamer (as a decoration), banderole (flat band with an inscription)
bannir — to banish. Also: to ban, to proscribe, to forbid, to prohibit
baptiser — to baptise. Also: to christen
baraque — shack, hut. Also: stall, stand, pad, crib (house), musclehead, muscleman, man built like a brick , bulky man (large and muscular man)

barbe — beard. Also: longer hair growing on the chin or face of some animals, barbel (whisker-like sensory organs, located around the mouth of certain fish), barb (feather), barb (hair or bristle), a boring thing, a drag
baril — barrel (volume used to measure petroleum and similar products).
barrage — dam, barrage. Also: barrier, roadblock, playoff

barre — bar, cake, ingot. Also: clipping of barre oblique: the slash mark ⟨/⟩, clipping of barre de fraction: the fraction slash ⟨⁄⟩, clipping of barre inscrite: the bar diacritics ⟨̵⟩, ⟨̶⟩, ⟨̷⟩, and ⟨̸⟩, clipping of barre verticale: the vertical bar ⟨|⟩, clipping of barre oblique inversée: the backslash ⟨\⟩, helm, tiller, bend sinister, erection, boner
basculer — to topple, to tip over. Also: to sway from side to side, to fall apart, to turn upsidedown, to transfer (e.g. a call), to tip over, to tip up, to fail over
baser — to base (have as its foundation or starting point).

basilique — covered building, Civil reunion place open to the public. Also: a Christian church building having a nave with a semicircular apse, side aisles, a narthex and a clerestory, a Roman Catholic church or cathedral with basilican status, an honorific status granted by the pope to recognize its historical, architectural, or sacramental importance
bateau-mouche — a tourist sightseeing-boat, used especially in Paris.
battant — a leaf (of a door or a window). Also: a leaf (of a foldable table or a counter), a stayer, a fighter, a go-getter (someone who shoots for success despite obstacles)
beau-père — a father-in-law, the father of one's spouse. Also: a stepfather, stepdad, the present husband of the mother of a child from a previous marriage
bec — beak, bill (of a bird). Also: mouthpiece; fipple, mouth, kiss
benne — bin, skip. Also: dump truck, barrow, carrying basket, cable car, telecabin
berge — bank (of river, canal). Also: raised pavement, banked edge
berline — berlin (four-wheeled carriage). Also: a sedan or saloon car, a minecart, tub
best-seller — bestseller (a top selling product, especially a book).
biais — means, way. Also: angle, aspect, way, bias, slant, diagonal line
bienfait — kindness, favour, good deed. Also: benefit, advantage
bilinguisme — bilingualism, the condition of being bilingual.
billard — billiards. Also: pool table, operating table
bille — marble (spherical ball). Also: ball, ball bearing, dimwit
biologique — biological. Also: biological (related by blood), organic (without pesticide)
biscotte — rusk (light, soft bread, often toasted or crisped in an oven). Also: yellow card, booking
bizarre — bizarre, odd, strange, funny. Also: peculiar, quaint
blanchir — to launder, wash. Also: to make white; to whiten, to grow or become white, to blanch or bleach, to launder, to whitewash (to cover over errors or bad actions)

bleu — the color blue. Also: blue cheese, rookie, new recruit, raw recruit, bruise, billiard chalk, a lightweight letter format intended for pneumatic delivery and later for airmail, a supporter of the Conservative Party, a supporter of the Democratic Party
bloc — a block (e.g., of wood). Also: a bloc, an alliance, a pad of paper, block (of memory, of code)
blocage — block (e.g. of traffic). Also: block (act of physically blocking), block (obstruction), block, blocking, blockage, locking (of brakes)

bloquer — to block (physically), to barricade, to obstruct. Also: to jam, to wedge, to fix in place, to freeze (e.g. prices), to defeat, to block, to impede, to hinder, to freeze, to lock up, to become jammed, to retreat
boiser — to panel (a room with wooden panels). Also: to afforest, to hit the ball with the frame (wood) of the racket

bombe — bomb (a device filled with explosives). Also: aerosol (either the substance or the container), a hottie, a bombshell, globular glass vessel; demijohn, carboy, bombe glacée, a frozen dessert consisting of two or more kinds of ice cream, often with a light, frothy center made of eggs and sugar, frozen in a melon-shaped mold, ellipsis of bombe météorologique, bombe météo, or bombe cyclonique
border — to border (add a border to). Also: to border (share a border with), to tuck in
bordure — border (the outer edge of something). Also: bordure, the foot of a sail, kerb (the edge of a pavement or sidewalk)

borne — bollard such as those used to restrict automobiles off a pedestrian area. Also: territorial boundary marker, territorial or geographical border, milestone such as those alongside a roadway, a kilometre; a click, mark, limit of a list or of an interval, machine
borner — to limit, confine. Also: to confine oneself (à faire to doing something), to content oneself
bosser — to emboss. Also: to dent; to cause a dent, to raise an anchor over the davit(s)
boucherie — butchery, butchering (profession of the butcher). Also: butchershop, bloodbath, slaughter, social event at which people gather to slaughter a pig
bouchon — cork, bung, stopper, plug. Also: float (in angling), traffic jam, dongle, restaurant, kid, mite, munchkin
boucler — to buckle (to fasten). Also: to curl (make curly), to tie up, to finish up (e.g. an affair or case), to shut, to curl up (become curly)
bouffer — to eat. Also: to eat, to worry, to consume in excess, to bash (criticise harshly)
bouffée — puff (of wind, smoke, etc.). Also: surge, rush
bouge — hovel; dive. Also: bulge, protuberance
boulet — cannonball. Also: ball and chain, fetlock, goof, goofball
bouleversant — heartrending, deeply moving, deeply touching, earth-shattering (very upsetting).
bouquin — old, worn-out book. Also: any book
bourreau — torturer. Also: executioner, hangman (the person), individual who lives extravagantly

bourrer — to stuff with stuffing material. Also: to completely fill by shoving in, to make to eat a lot, to force-feed, to hit someone, to get drunk, to binge, to jam by way of excessive accumulation, to rush
bourse — coin purse. Also: a purseful of money; by extension, any sum of money available to be paid, financial grant, bourse, stock exchange, the scrotum, balls
boursier — grantee, scholarship beneficiary, recipient of a bursary. Also: stockbroker, pursemaker
bousculer — to shove, to bump into, to jostle.

brasserie — brewery (a place where beer is brewed). Also: brewery (a beer-producing company), brasserie (a restaurant), beer bar, beer parlor / beer parlour, alehouse (a pub or bar where beer is sold)
braver — to challenge, defy. Also: to brave, to flout, pay no heed to (someone's opinion, hostility, etc.)
bricoler — to do DIY. Also: to tinker (to fiddle with something in an attempt to fix, mend or improve it)

brimade — a hazing, some test which seniors impose on newbies in (para) military units, school, etc. by way of initiation. Also: a punishment which an educator imposes on a child, consisting in some privation, a measure or treatment which is pointlessly hurtful
brin — blade (of grass). Also: sprig, twig, wisp, strand (of hair, fibre etc.), ounce, bit, hint
brique — brick (hardened block used for building). Also: carton box (food packaging), doorstop (thick, massive book, large book), ten thousand French francs (one million old francs, ~1524 euros)
briquet — firestriker. Also: cigarette lighter, lighter, beagle, firesteel, frizzen
brocante — synonym of marché aux puces: flea market; market for bric-à-brac. Also: car boot sale; jumble sale
broche — brooch, pin. Also: spit, skewer, spike, peg
bronzer — to bronze (plate with bronze). Also: to tan, bronze, to tan, bronze, catch a tan
brosse — brush (the implement). Also: crew cut
brosser — to brush (to clean/tidy/shape with a brush). Also: to whip, to beat (to defeat), to play truant, to skip class
brouillon — draft (early version of a written work).
brusque — abrupt (sudden or hasty). Also: curt
bruyère — heather, heath, briar. Also: a place of briars, a moor
brève — a brief piece of information or news (as opposed to the headline). Also: short vowel, breve, pitta; any bird belonging to the passerine family Pittidae
buisson — bush. Also: brush, thicket (wild vegetation)
business — business, firm, company. Also: business, affairs
buter — to stop dead. Also: to prop up, buttress, to kill, to obsess; to become obsessed
butte — small hill, mound, hillock; knoll. Also: heap, a mound of dirt upon which targets were placed to practice shooting, butt, target
buveur — drinker (someone who drinks, especially alcohol).
bâillement — yawn (the action of yawning). Also: large gap
bâtir — to build; to construct. Also: to build up
bénéfice — profit, earning. Also: benefit, advantage
bénéficier — to enjoy, to benefit from, to get, to receive. Also: to benefit to
bénéfique — beneficial, that which does good.
bénévole — voluntary, unpaid (working or done without payment).
béquille — kickstand. Also: crutch (device to assist in motion as a cane)
cabaret — pub, tavern. Also: cabaret (restaurant or nightclub with live entertainment), tray (object on which things are carried), asarabacca
cadavre — corpse, cadaver. Also: empty bottle, empty
cadet — cadet, student officer. Also: junior sportsperson, young player, a younger sibling

vernissage — varnishing, glazing. Also: vernissage (private viewing of an art exhibition before it opens to the public)
verrière — a glass skylight. Also: female equivalent of verrier
verrou — bolt. Also: lock, lock, mutex
verrouiller — to bolt (lock with a bolt). Also: to lock (to prevent something, e.g. a button or keypad, from performing its task)
versement — payment, especially an electronic transaction.
verser — to pour. Also: to pay, to put money into a fund, to overturn (turn over, capsize)

vestiaire — cloakroom; (US) checkroom, coatroom, coat check (a room intended for holding guests' cloaks and other heavy outerwear, as at a theater or night club). Also: locker room, changing room, vestry
vestibule — hall, entrance hall. Also: vestibule
veuf — widowed. Also: odd (missing its pair)
vidange — an act of emptying. Also: oil change (the emptying and replacing of engine oil in a vehicle), garbage, trash
vide — empty. Also: devoid, blank (page, tape), vacant; unfurnished (apartment)
vider — to empty, to empty out. Also: to gut (e.g. a fish)
vieillissement — aging, the action of getting older.
vierge — virgin. Also: blank (of paper, recording/storage media, etc.)
vilain — ugly. Also: disagreeable, awful, naughty, villainous, wicked, nasty, evil
villageois — villagelike, characteristic of a village.
virage — bend, turn, curve. Also: change, shift (in orientation), turn, change in colour
virer — to bear, veer (change direction slightly). Also: to turn into, become, to transfer (money, from one account to another), to get rid of, fire

vis-à-vis — a meeting, especially a private one. Also: a position where two things face each other, an equivalent, a counterpart on the opposing side, interlocutor, negotiation partner, What faces someone or something, such as a view or the person seated in front, a type of S-shaped couch or sofa that allows people to be seated face-to-face
visa — a mark or stamp attesting to the performance of an official action. Also: a travel visa
viser — to aim, to target. Also: to watch, to stare
vitrail — stained glass. Also: stained glass window
vitrine — shop window. Also: shopping, vitrine (glass-paneled cabinet or case for displaying articles)
voisinage — proximity, vicinity. Also: neighbourly relations, neighbourhood
voiturer — to transport. Also: to drive, chauffeur (transport by vehicle)
volaille — poultry. Also: po-po, bacon (police), a freshman in a military school, a thievish prostitute
volant — steering wheel. Also: flywheel, flounce, shuttlecock, birdie
volontaire — voluntary, deliberate. Also: volunteer, determined, wilful
volubile — talkative (talking a great deal with ease, and quickly changing subjects). Also: inconstant, changeable, variable
volupté — voluptuousness. Also: rich and intense pleasure, especially sexual pleasure
vouer — to vow. Also: to devote (+ à to), to devote oneself, to be devoted

vouloir — to want, wish, desire. Also: to expect; to think; forms rhetorical questions, perhaps better translated to English by rephrasing with would or should, to see oneself as; to give the impression of, to seem
vouvoyer — to address someone using the formal pronoun vous rather than the informal tu, to use the V-form, to "you" (as opposed to "to "thou"").
voyant — clairvoyant. Also: showy, gaudy, conspicuous, staring
voyou — urchin, street child. Also: thug, yob, hooligan, good for nothing
vécu — experience (what one experiences). Also: inner life
véhiculer — to transport in a vehicle, to carry. Also: to promote, to convey (a message), to carry
vénitien — Venetian (of, from or relating to the city of Venice in the Veneto region, Italy).
vêtir — to dress, to clothe. Also: to dress, get dressed
zébrer — to stripe, to stripe like a zebra.
éboueur — dustman, garbage man, garbage collector (person employed to collect refuse from people's homes and take it to be processed).
ébranler — to shake, rattle. Also: to weaken (health), disturb, unhinge (spirits), to move off, set off (of vehicle)
écaille — scale (of fish, reptile etc.). Also: shell (of tortoise, oyster), tortoiseshell (manufacturing material)
écarquiller — to open wide, to goggle.

écarter — to separate, move apart. Also: to spread, open (fingers, legs etc), to draw (curtains), to dismiss, rule out, turn down, to remove a person from a job or position, to lose, to withdraw, diverge, deviate, to stray
échelle — ladder. Also: scale, proportion, size, scale
échelon — rung. Also: echelon, soup bone
écho — echo (a reflected sound that is heard again by its initial observer). Also: rumour
échouer — to fail, fall through, miscarry. Also: to end, wind up, to ground, run aground, to beach
échéance — expiry date, expiration date. Also: due date, redemption date, date of payment, term (period of time)
éclaircir — to brighten (up). Also: to thin (down), to clear up, solve, clarify (mystery, problem etc.)
éclat — brilliance, shine, lustre. Also: fragment, Strong reaction; scandal, clap, peal (of thunder, laughter), tinder, kindling
éclectique — eclectic (selecting a mixture of what appear to be best of various doctrines, methods or styles).
écolier — (university) student (especially in the Middle Ages). Also: schoolboy
écologique — ecologic, ecological. Also: ecologically friendly, environmentally friendly, sustainable, green
écologiste — environmentalist (one who advocates for the protection of the environment). Also: ecologist
écoper — to bail out. Also: to get, to be on the receiving end of
écorce — bark (of a tree). Also: peel (of certain fruits), crust (of the Earth)
écorcher — to skin (to remove the skin off). Also: to graze (one's body), to mispronounce, butcher
écossais — Scottish Gaelic language. Also: Scots language

écumer — to foam, foam up, froth, froth up. Also: to lather (as of a horse), to unfoam, remove foam from, to take the scum off, to skim, to scour (shops, etc., looking for something), to grift (to skim off profits etc.)
écuyer — squire, equerry. Also: rider, horseman
édit — an edict, type of legislative and/or judicial proclamation, originally emanating from a Roman magistrate.
éditer — to publish. Also: to edit (be the editor of), edit (to change a text, or a document)
égalité — equality. Also: deuce, tie (in sport)
égarer — to misplace. Also: to mislead, misinform, to lead astray, to get lost, go astray, to lose one's way; to wander
égoïsme — egoism (tendency to think of self).
égrener — to shell, pod (maize, peas, wheat etc.). Also: to gin (cotton), to ripple (flax), to drop off the stalk or bunch, to dish out, to rattle off, go through (a list), tick away (time)
élaboration — drawing up, putting together, creation.
élaborer — to draw up, write up, make up, whip up, put together. Also: to process farther, to produce, to develop, to metabolize, to elaborate, to work out
élancer — to dash forward, to throw oneself at. Also: to soar up
élargir — to enlarge, to make larger.
électromécanique — electromechanical (combining the fields of mechanics and electronics).
électron — electron (the negatively charged subatomic particles that orbit atoms).
élevage — livestock farming. Also: farm, stock (of animals), maturing (of wine, in barrels)
éliminer — to eliminate; to phase out.
éloignement — distancing (putting something further away). Also: removal, distance between two things, long time ago, long distance, distance (lack of intimacy)
éloquence — eloquence (the quality of artistry and persuasiveness in speech or writing).
éloquent — eloquent (fluently persuasive and articulate). Also: eloquent; descriptive
élu — elected official, politician (person who has been elected in a vote). Also: chosen (person who has been chosen by God)
élucider — clarify, elucidate. Also: solve, resolve
émailler — to enamel. Also: to mar, spoil, blemish
émergence — emergence. Also: emergence (the arising of emergent structure in complex systems)
émergent — emerging (in the process of emerging).
émerger — to arrive at the top; to move upwards. Also: emerge (to come into view), to wake up
émigrer — to emigrate. Also: to migrate
énigme — enigma, puzzle (anything difficult to understand or make sense of). Also: riddle
énoncer — to enounce, lay out, declare. Also: to explain
énumérer — to enumerate; count out; go through one by one.
éolien — Aeolian. Also: Aeolic, wind, aeolian
épanouir — to blossom; to bloom; to flower. Also: to reveal its qualities, to thrive, flourish
épargner — to save (time, money etc.). Also: to save, spare (someone from something)
épauler — to give a hand, to help out.
éperon — spur (for riding or on a cockerel). Also: spur (mountain that shoots from another mountain or range), ram; cutwater
éphémère — ephemeral (lasting for only a short time; transitory). Also: existing for only one day; ephemeral
épinard — spinach plant (Spinacia oleracea). Also: spinach (foodstuff)
épopée — epic, saga. Also: adventurous and intrepid journey
éprendre — to burn. Also: to become enamoured with, fall in love with
éprouver — to put to the test, test, try. Also: to feel, experience, to experience, go through
épuiser — to dry up. Also: to squeeze out, to exhaust, to wear out
équation — equation (assertion that two expressions are equal, expressed by writing the two expressions separated by an equal sign).
équilibrer — to balance, make balanced, to equilibrize. Also: to counterbalance
équivaloir — to be equivalent. Also: to be equivalent, to be the same

étaler — to spread, spread out (papers, objects, etc.; sur on or over). Also: to spread, smear (butter, etc.), to spread, stagger (repayments), to flaunt, show off (talents, money), to spread out, stretch out, to show off, to fall over
étang — pond (small lake, often shallow). Also: lagoon (especially on the Mediterranean coast)
étape — stage, stop (on a journey). Also: leg (of race), step, stage

étendre — to spread, to spread out, to lay out. Also: to stretch (the limbs), to hang out (washing), to extend, to enlarge, expand, to expand upon; to discuss in greater detail, to dilute; to water down
étendue — expanse, area (of land, water etc.). Also: size (of a country, collection etc.), scale, range, extent (of knowledge, damage etc.); depth (of ignorance)
étinceler — to spark (give off sparks). Also: to sparkle, twinkle
étirement — stretch (act of stretching, all senses).
étirer — to stretch. Also: to stretch (stretch one's muscles)
étoiler — to scatter or adorn with stars. Also: synonym of consteller
étourdir — to daze, to stun. Also: to tire, to wear out
étrangler — to strangle. Also: to choke (with laughter, etc.), to say in a voice strangled by emotion.
étrenne — the first sale of the day made by a shopkeeper, merchant etc.. Also: the first time something is used/worn/done, New Year's gift; Christmas present (especially when talking to a child)
évacuation — evacuation (act of emptying). Also: a drain (for a sink, shower, etc.)
évader — to escape (from a building, situation etc.).
éventail — fan (hand-held device). Also: range, array
éventuel — possible, hypothetical (that might come about).
éventuellement — possibly, maybe, perhaps; if you want. Also: eventually

ôter — to take away, remove. Also: to take off, remove (clothes, etc.), to remove, cut (text etc.); to take away (in arithmetic), to take (something) away from someone; to deprive, to move oneself, get out of the way
